coredata.dc:title: Facile, green and clean one-step synthesis of carbon dots from wool: Application as a sensor for glyphosate detection based on the inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Long
Bi, Yidan
Hou, Juan
Li, Huiyu
Xu, Yuan
Wang, Bo
Ding, Hong
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we reported a green route for the fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). Wool, a kind of nontoxic and natural raw material, was chosen as the precursor to prepare CDs via a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis process. Compared with previously reported methods for preparation of CDs based on biomass materials, this method was simple, facile and free of any additives, such as acids, bases, or salts, which avoid the complicated post-treatment process to purify the CDs. The CDs have a high quantum yield (16.3%) and their fluorescence could be quenched by silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) based on inner filter effect (IFE). The presence of glyphosate could induce the aggregation of AgNPs and thus result in the fluorescence recovery of the quenched CDs. Based on this phenomenon, we constructed a fluorescence system (CDs/AgNPs) for determination of glyphosate. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/AgNPs system was proportional to the concentration of glyphosate in the range of 0.025–2.5μgmL−1, with a detection limit of 12ngmL−1. Furthermore, the established method has been successfully used for glyphosate detection in the cereal samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Wool
Microwave
Inner filter effect
Glyphosate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental
Chemicals and materials
Instrumentation
Preparation of the CDs
Synthesis of cysteamine stabilized Ag NPs
Procedures for the detection of glyphosate
Detection of glyphosate in cereal samples
Results and discussion
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
Effects of AgNPs on the fluorescence of CDs
Effects of glyphosate on the absorption spectrum of AgNPs
Fluorescence detection of glyphosate based on the CDs-AgNPs system
Optimization of the experimental conditions for the detection of glyphosate
The sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of glyphosate
Detection of glyphosate in cereal samples
Conclusion
Acknowledgments
Supplementary material
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the CDs (magnification ×145,000). Inset: HRTEM image of the CDs (magnification ×880,000). The accelerating voltage was 200kV. (B) The size distribution of the CDs. (C) XRD pattern of the CDs (Cu Kα radiation, 200mA, 40kV, 0.02° step scanning and 10°min−1 scan speed). (D) FT-IR spectrum of the CDs (32 scans at 2cm−1 resolution in the scanning range of 400–4000cm−1). (E) XPS survey spectra of the CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (F), N1s (G) and S2p (H).

(A) UV−vis absorption spectrum (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) at different excitation wavelengths of the CDs (2.5 μg mL−1). Inset: Photograph of the CDs under illumination of white light (left) and UV (365nm) light (right). (B) fluorescence excitation (a) and emission (c) spectra of the CDs and UV−vis absorption spectrum of AgNPs (b).

Effects of different pH (A) and concentrations of AgNPs (B) on F/F 0 for the detection of 1.5μgmL−1 glyphosate. (C) A 402-time variation curve of 2.25nM AgNPs in the presence of 1.5μgmL−1 glyphosate. CDs, 2.5μgmL−1; AgNPs, 2.25nM; pH=4.0. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgNPs system in the presence and absence of glyphosate, respectively.

Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-AgNPs system in the presence of different concentrations of glyphosate (λex=360nm). From a to f: 0, 0.025, 0.25, 0.5, 1.5, 2.5μgmL−1. AgNPs: 2.25nM; pH: 4.0. Inset: the linear calibration plot of F/F0 versus glyphosate concentration. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgNPs system in the presence and absence of glyphosate, respectively.

The selectivity of the CDs-AgNPs system to various pesticides (0.1μgmL−1) in the acetate buffer (pH 4.0). F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgNPs system in the presence and absence of pesticides, respectively.
Illustration of the fluorescent detection of glyphosate through the IFE of AgNPs on the CDs.
Analytical results for the determination of glyphosate in spiked cereal samples with different aging time.

head.ce:title: Facile, green and clean one-step synthesis of carbon dots from wool: Application as a sensor for glyphosate detection based on the inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we reported a green route for the fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). Wool, a kind of nontoxic and natural raw material, was chosen as the precursor to prepare CDs via a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis process. Compared with previously reported methods for preparation of CDs based on biomass materials, this method was simple, facile and free of any additives, such as acids, bases, or salts, which avoid the complicated post-treatment process to purify the CDs. The CDs have a high quantum yield (16.3%) and their fluorescence could be quenched by silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) based on inner filter effect (IFE). The presence of glyphosate could induce the aggregation of AgNPs and thus result in the fluorescence recovery of the quenched CDs. Based on this phenomenon, we constructed a fluorescence system (CDs/AgNPs) for determination of glyphosate. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/AgNPs system was proportional to the concentration of glyphosate in the range of 0.025–2.5μgmL−1, with a detection limit of 12ngmL−1. Furthermore, the established method has been successfully used for glyphosate detection in the cereal samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate (N-[phosphonmethyl]glycine) is a postemergence, broad-spectrum, and non-selective herbicide that exhibits excellent performance and effect in weed control [1]. Due to its relatively low toxicity to mammals, glyphosate has become the most frequently used herbicide all around the world [2]. However, the indiscriminate application of glyphosate may easily lead to high-level residues in agricultural products, which can create a potential threat to human health [3]. The median lethal dose (LD50) of glyphosate in rats is around 5000mgkg−1 [4]. Although the acute toxicity of glyphosate for rats is relatively low, the additives and surfactants in commercial formulations can improve the absorbance of glyphosate and thus increase the toxicity [4]. Recently, the glyphosate has been classified as probably carcinogenic to humans [5]. Therefore, it is of a significant importance to establish a reliable method for the determination of glyphosate in agricultural samples.

Up to now, numerous analytical methods have been developed for glyphosate detection, such as gas chromatography (GC) [6–8], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [9,10], and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11,12]. However, derivatization procedures have to be performed to improve the detection sensitivity of glyphosate, due to the low volatility, high water solubility, high polarity and absence of chromophore or fluorophore groups in the molecular structure. Other detection methods for glyphosate have been reported, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [13], ion chromatography (IC) [14] and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [15]. These methods show high sensitivity and good accuracy, but they often require time-consuming and complicated steps, expensive instrumentations and specific skills for operation, which limit their practical application. Therefore, developing simple, low-cost, rapid and sensitive method for the determination of glyphosate has become a pressing demand.

Among the various detection techniques, fluorescence detection provides many advantages such as rapid response, high sensitivity, simplicity, and ease of operation. A fluorescent method has also been established for glyphosate determination [2] based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CdTe quantum dots and gold nanoparticles. However, the raw materials for preparation of CdTe quantum dots were expensive and the synthetic process was relatively complex, which may limit their wider applications.

Recently, photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CDs) have received great attention, due to their high resistance to photobleaching, high aqueous solubility, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and robust chemical inertness [16–18]. Up to now, tremendous effort has been made to synthesize CDs with excellent properties through different ways or materials. Compared with conventional chemicals, biomass materials exhibit amazing advances in the fabrication of CDs, as they are easy to obtain, inexpensive and nontoxic. More recently, many biomass materials have been used to prepare CDs, such as hair fiber [19,20] , spider silk [21] , silk [22] , apple juice [23] , potato [24], protein [25], etc. However, these methods suffered from various drawbacks including time-consuming procedures, low quantum yields, addition of strong acid or alkali. In this work, we reported a simple, facile and green route to synthesize photoluminescent CDs from wool by a one-step microwave method without any additives, such as acids, bases, or salts. The resultant CDs can be directly applied as fluorescent probes only through simple centrifugation and filter.

The inner filter effect (IFE) refers to the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light of fluorophores by absorbers in the detection system [26]. The recent studies have explored the application of IFE in developing novel fluorescent methods for analyte detections [26–32]. In this study, we designed a new fluorescent probe for glyphosate detection based on the IFE of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for the fluorescence of CDs. To construct an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of glyphosate, high dispersion and uniform size distribution of AgNPs were important for this work. The principle of our experimental design is illustrated in Scheme 1. The fluorescence intensity of CDs can be quenched by AgNPs based on inner-filter effect (IFE). However, in the presence of glyphosate, negatively charged glyphosate can induce the aggregation of positively charged AgNPs by electrostatic interaction. The absorbance decreases of AgNPs led to the fluorescence recovery of the quenched CDs. Therefore, a highly sensitive fluorescence sensing system for glyphosate detection was developed. And this sensing system has been successfully applied to detect glyphosate in cereal samples with good precision and accuracy.

Wool was obtained from a local sheep farm. Silver nitrate, cysteamine hydrochloride, sodium borohydride were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate, concentrated sulfuric acid, anhydrous sodium acetate and acetic acid were obtained from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). All reagents in this experiment were of analytical reagent grade without further purification. Glyphosate, glufosinate, metsulfuron-methyl, metaflumizone, azoxystrobin, chlorothalonil, deltamethrin, alphamethrin, acetochlor, dimethoate, malathion, phorate, methamidophos, thiodicarb, chlorfenapyr, chlorpyrifos, dinotefuran, pymetrozine and imidacloprid were obtained from Jiangsu Pesticide Research Institute (Jiangsu, China). The water used in all experiments was obtained from a Milli-Q water system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) and the resistivity was 18.2MΩcm at 25°C.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR, Bruker, Germany) were preformed on a Nicolet Impact 410 FTIR spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets. UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a UV-2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence spectroscopy was carried out on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The absolute quantum yield (QY) was obtained using an integrating sphere on a FLS 920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, Japan) pattern of CDs was recorded on a Rigaku D/max-2500 diffractometer with a graphite monochromator by using Cu Kα radiation operating at 200mA and 40kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) was obtained on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscope using Mg Kα X-ray as the excitation source. The size and morphology of the CDs were examined by using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Company, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Microwave digestion instrument (MDS-6) was purchased from Shanghai Xinyi Company (Shanghai, China).

The wool was first washed with water several times and cut into 3–5mm in length. In a typical procedure, the wool (0.3g) was mixed with 40mL of H2O, then added into a microwave digestion tank and heated at 200°C for 60min by microwave irradiation. After the microwave digestion, the tank was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 15min to remove the large particles. Subsequently, the suspension was filtered through a 0.22µm microporous membrane. Finally, a clear yellow suspension containing CDs was collected for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cysteamine stabilized Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The AgNPs were synthesized according to a literature-reported procedure with some modifications [33]. All glassware used in this experiment were cleaned in a bath of freshly prepared concentrated HCl/HNO3 (3:1, v/v) and thoroughly rinsed with water prior to use. Briefly, 6mL of 0.3% of cysteamine hydrochloride was added to an aqueous solution containing 40mL of 0.1mM AgNO3 which was placed in an ice bath with vigorous stirring. After 20min, 30mL of 0.4mM freshly prepared ice-cold NaBH4 solution was dropwise added into the above solution under vigorous stirring. Then, the mixture was further stirred for 60min at room temperature. The resulting yellow colloidal solution was filtered by 0.22µm filter and stored at 4°C for further use. The morphology and size of the AgNPs were characterized with TEM. The molar extinction coefficient (ε) for the AgNPs is about 6.9×10−8 M−1 cm−1 [28]. Thus, the molar concentration of the AgNPs was estimated to be 15nM according to Lambert–Beer's law.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for the detection of glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, 300μL AgNPs (15nM) solution and 100μL of 20mM acetate buffer solution (pH=4.0) were mixed with different concentrations of glyphosate. Then, the mixture was diluted to 1.9mL with water and incubated for 12min at room temperature. After that, 100μL of 50μgmL−1 CDs was added into the above solution and mixed thoroughly. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were measured at 445nm with the excitation wavelength of 360nm. The calibration curve for glyphosate was set up by F0/F versus the concentration of glyphosate, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the system in the presence and absence of glyphosate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in cereal samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was used to determine the content of glyphosate in rice, millet, wheat flour and maize flour samples purchased from a local supermarket. The rice and millet samples were grounded into powders. The cereal samples (1g) were mixed with 20mL water and 10mL dichloromethane. The mixture was sonicated for 15min and centrifuged for 10min at 4500rpm. The collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22µm microporous membrane. Finally, the filtrate was evaporated to 2mL and the glyphosate concentration was detected by the proposed method.

The cereal samples were also analyzed by a reference method in order to insure the reliability of the proposed method, in which glyphosate was detected using high-performance liquid chromatography after derivatization with 4-chloro-3,5-dinitrobenzotrifluoride as described in Ref. [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, several multi-step methods have been used to synthesize CDs, such as the acidic oxidization of carbohydrates and the further surface passivation with amine-terminated compounds [35], the pyrolyzing polymer/F127/silica composite and subsequent etching with NaOH [36], the carbonization and etching of hair fiber with concentrated H2SO4 and subsequent adjustment of pH with NaOH [19]. In this work, we successfully prepared the CDs via a one-step microwave pyrolysis method. Compared with the multi-step methods, the one-step synthesis strategy was simple, facile, time-saving and did not require complex manipulation. In addition, the one-step reaction process in this study provided a low-cost and green method for the preparation of CDs using natural precursor as the raw material. There were no toxic chemicals used during the synthesis process. Thus, we presented a non-toxic, economic and eco-friendly route for the preparation of CDs.

The morphology of the as-prepared CDs was characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, the TEM image of the CDs reveals that the CDs are nearly spherical and well separated from each other. The sizes of the CDs are mainly distributed in the range of 1.5–4.5nm with an average size of 2.8nm (Fig. 1B). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 1A) indicates that the CDs have a lattice spacing of 0.201nm, which corresponds to the (102) diffraction planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [37].

The XRD profiles of the CDs (Fig. 1C) show a wider diffraction peak located at around 20.8 degrees, suggesting that the interlayer spacing of the (002) diffraction peak is 0.42nm. The interlayer spacing is bigger than that of graphite (0.34nm), which can be attributed to the introduction of oxygen-containing groups in the synthesis process [38]. FT-IR spectra were performed to indentify the functional groups on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 1D). As revealed in Table S1, there exist O–H, N–H, S–H, C=O, C–OH, C=S and C–O groups on the surface of CDs [39,40].

XPS was employed to examine the surface states and compositions of the CDs. The XPS survey spectrum of the CDs shows four typical peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s and inconspicuous S2p (Fig. 1E). The fitted results of C1s, N1s and S2p spectra are shown in Table S2. The high-resolution C1s spectrum indicates the presence of C–C, C–O and C=N/C=O groups (Fig. 1F), which are consistent with the FTIR data. The N1s spectrum shown in Fig. 1G indicates that nitrogen exists mostly in the form of C–N–C, N–C3 and N–H. Besides, five fitted peaks are revealed in the Fig. 1H, which can be ascribed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the –C–S– covalent bonds of the thiophene-S for their spin-orbit couplings, and the –C–SOx– (x=2, 3, 4) [19,41]. The characterization from FTIR and XPS demonstrate that the surface of the CDs is functionalized by nitrogen-, sulfur-, and oxygen-containing groups. These functional groups can improve the stability and hydrophilicity of the CDs in an aqueous system, which is beneficial to the applications of CDs in aqueous samples.

The UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra were performed to investigate the optical properties of the CDs. The UV–Vis spectrum of the CDs shows a clear adsorption peak at 270nm (curve a in Fig. 2A), which can be ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond [42]. Under the excitation at 365nm, the light yellow CDs solution exhibits strong blue luminescence (inset in Fig. 2A). The CDs solution exhibits excitation-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence properties with emission peaks ranging from 435nm to 480nm at excitation from 320nm to 420nm (curve b in Fig. 2A). The behavior is considered to be related to different sizes and a distribution of different surface states of carbon nanoparticles [43]. The absolute QY of the resultant CDs dispersed in water was measured to be about 16.3% (excitation wavelength, 360nm), which is higher than most of the biomass-generated CDs previously reported [44–47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of AgNPs on the fluorescence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Fig. S1, that the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs is efficiently quenched with increasing the concentration of AgNPs in solution. The possible quenching mechanism was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 2B, the AgNPs present a broad absorption spectrum from 300nm to 600nm, with a strong absorption peak at 402nm. Meanwhile, the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the CDs aqueous solution are 360 and 450nm, respectively. Therefore, the absorption spectrum of AgNPs shows quite precise overlapping with the excitation and emission bands of CDs. So, the FRET or IFE may occur between the AgNPs and CDs in this present system.

In order to ascertain the main reason for the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectra of AgNPs in the presence and absence of CDs were measured. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorption spectrum of AgNPs remains unchanged in the presence of CDs, which indicates that no complex was formed and FRET between AgNPs and CDs did not occur [27]. At the same time, the zeta potential was employed to measure the surface charges of AgNPs and CDs in acetate buffer solution (pH=4.0). The zeta potential of AgNPs and CDs were measured to be +18.7mV and +15.4mV, respectively. Thus, there was electrostatic repulsion between positively charged AgNPs and positively charged CDs. The distance between AgNPs and CDs should be greater than 10nm, therefore, the FRET between AgNPs and CDs cannot occur [27,48]. To further confirm the quenching mechanism, we explored the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs in the presence of different analytes and the fluorescence decay of CDs was fitted using a three-exponential decay function (Fig. S3). The average lifetimes of CDs, CDs+AgNPs, and CDs+AgNPs+glyphosate were measured to be 7.86, 7.67 and 7.78ns, respectively. The results indicated that the average lifetime of CDs was hardly changed in the presence of AgNPs and glyphosate, thereby excluding the possibility of the FRET process between the AgNPs and CDs. Therefore, all these results collectively demonstrate that the observed fluorescence quenching should be mainly attributed to the IFE of AgNPs on the fluorescence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of glyphosate on the absorption spectrum of AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the characteristic absorption peak of AgNPs is very sensitive to their interparticle distance. The monodisperse AgNPs will approach each other and tend to aggregate upon external inducement such as hydrogen bond interaction, electrostatic interaction, and donor–acceptor chemical reactions between target analytes and AgNPs [49]. In consequence, the interparticle distance of AgNPs gradually decrease and the characteristic absorption peaks display a distinct change [49]. As shown in Fig. S4A, the absorption of AgNPs at 402nm exhibits an obvious decrease with the increasing concentration of glyphosate in the AgNPs solution. The AgNPs were positively charged because of the superficial –NH3 + groups, resulting in high stability against aggregation due to the electrostatic repulsion force. As shown in Scheme 1, negative charged glyphosate is inclined to adsorb onto the surface of positively charged AgNPs by electrostatic interactions, resulting in the aggregation of AgNPs. The glyphosate-induced aggregation of AgNPs was further confirmed by the TEM observations. The AgNPs are dispersed with an average size of about 13.1nm in the absence of glyphosate (Fig. S4B). However, the significant aggregation of AgNPs is observed in the presence of glyphosate (Fig. S4C). The results of the TEM are consistent with the change of the UV–visible absorption spectra.
Therefore, the fluorescence emission of CDs can be tuned by the AgNPs and glyphosate, which provides a new method for fluorescent detection of glyphosate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of glyphosate based on the CDs-AgNPs system
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the expected principle for glyphosate detection, the effect of glyphosate on the CDs-AgNPs system has been investigated (Fig. S5). The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs show no obvious difference in the absence (curve a in Fig. S5) and presence (curve b in Fig. S5) of 10μgmL−1 glyphosate. The results indicate that there was no direct interaction between glyphosate and CDs. The fluorescence of CDs is effectively quenched (curve c in Fig. S5) due to the IFE with adding AgNPs into the CDs solution. In the presence of low concentration of glyphosate (0.05μgmL−1), a slight fluorescence recovery of CDs is observed in the fluorescence spectrum (curve d in Fig. S5). The IFE-decreased fluorescence of CDs is recovered obviously (curve e in Fig. S5) after addition of a high concentration of glyphosate (1.5μgmL−1). Meanwhile, there was no discernible change in the shape of the emission spectra of CDs, indicating that the recovered emission came from CDs rather than any other newly formed emission centers. As discussed above, glyphosate could induce the absorbance decrease of AgNPs at 402nm which diminished the IFE of AgNPs on CDs and then recovered the fluorescence of CDs. Thus, a novel, IFE-based fluorescent strategy for rapid determination of glyphosate was developed according to the turn-on response of glyphosate to the fluorescence of CDs quenched by AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for the detection of glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental parameters including the media pH, AgNPs concentration and interaction time were optimized for the determination of glyphosate. The glyphosate is a weak polyprotic acid and the pH value will greatly affect the form of glyphosate in aqueous solution. Therefore, the media pH is an important factor in the determination of glyphosate. The influence of different pH on F/F0 (F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgNPs system in the presence and absence of glyphosate) for the detection of 1.5μgmL−1 glyphosate was investigated in the pH range from 3 to 7 ( Fig. 3A). The maximum value of F/F0 was obtained at pH 4.0. In strong acid media (pH<4.0), the glyphosate was protonated and could not decrease the surface charge density of AgNPs or cross-link the neighboring AgNPs, which could not induce the aggregation of the AgNPs. At relatively high pH levels (pH≥5.5), the ionic strength of solution increased with the increasing pH value and the positive charges on AgNPs surface decreased, which slightly aggravated the aggregation of AgNPs in blank and led to the decrease of F/F0 value [50]. Thus, the optimal pH was chosen to be 4.0.

The influence of the AgNPs concentration on the fluorescence recovery of CDs was also studied. As shown in Fig. 3B, the optimal concentration of AgNPs is 2.25nM. A high concentration of AgNPs could quench the fluorescence of CDs more effectively and decrease the background fluorescence, but the response for glyphosate in the low concentration range was weak. A low concentration of AgNPs could improve the response for glyphosate in the low concentration range, but the corresponding background fluorescence would be too strong to display a wider linear range.

The effect of incubation time was further discussed. As shown in Fig. 3C, when 1.5μgmL−1 glyphosate was added, the absorbance of AgNPs at 402nm gradually decreased as time went on and then reached a minimum in 12min. The IFE can be generally considered as a kind of instant effect. Therefore, the incubation time of glyphosate-AgNPs binding was chosen to be 12min, and no extra incubation was needed after the addition of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: Under the above-mentioned optimal conditions, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of glyphosate are shown in Fig. 4. The fluorescence emission intensity of CDs increases gradually with the increment of glyphosate concentration. A good linear relationship between F/F0 and glyphosate concentration (the inset in Fig. 4) was observed in the range from 0.025 to 2.5μgmL−1 (R2=0.993). The limit of detection (LOD, 3S/N) and limit of quantification (LOQ, 10S/N) were measured to be 12ngmL−1 and 40ngmL−1, respectively. The LOD is lower or at least comparable to the values of previously reported methods listed in Table S3 [34,50–53]. To assess the repeatability of the proposed method, the AgNPs and CDs were prepared eleven times under the same conditions. Then, each batch of AgNPs and CDs were used to measure 0.1μgmL−1 glyphosate. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 4.1% for eleven repeated measurements of glyphosate, which indicated the good repeatability of the proposed method for glyphosate detection. Compared with previously reported methods for glyphosate detection [1–15,34,50–53], the proposed method exhibited many special advantages, such as low-cost raw materials, eco-friendly fluorescence sensor, simple sample preparation process and rapid fluorescence response time. In addition, the proposed method required no derivatization and expensive equipments and thus demonstrated great potential in real glyphosate analysis.

In order to explore the selectivity of the proposed approach, we investigated the effects of different pesticides on the detection of glyphosate, such as glufosinate, metsulfuron-methyl, metaflumizone, azoxystrobin, chlorothalonil, deltamethrin, alphamethrin, acetochlor, dimethoate, malathion, phorate, methamidophos, thiodicarb, chlorfenapyr, chlorpyrifos, dinotefuran, pymetrozine and imidacloprid. As shown in Fig. 5, glyphosate can obviously recover the IFE-decreased fluorescence of CDs, whereas the influences of other pesticides at the identical concentration (0.1μgmL−1) are negligible.

Compared with other pesticides mentioned above, the glyphosate molecule possesses functional groups of carboxyl (–COOH) and phosphonyl (–PO3H2). The glyphosate can be adsorbed on the surface of AgNPs by the electrostatic interaction between negatively charged groups (–COOH and –PO3H2 ) and positively charged amino groups (–NH2) under the experimental condition of pH=4. The glyphosate adsorbed on the surface of AgNPs could not only lead to a decrease of the average surface charge of AgNPs effectively but also cross-link the neighbor AgNPs by two equivalent sites (–COOH and –PO3H2), resulting in the aggregation of AgNPs [50]. It is worth noting that the glufosinate could not recover the IFE-decreased fluorescence of CDs. A possible reason is that the glufosinate could not bridge up the neighbor AgNPs smoothly due to the static resistance of methyl of glufosinate and the electrostatic repulsion force between –NH3 + groups [2]. Therefore, both the decrease of surface charge density of AgNPs and the cross-linking of the neighbor AgNPs may contribute to the high selectivity for glyphosate detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in cereal samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicality of the proposed method in real samples detection, the developed method was applied to glyphosate detection in cereal samples. There was no glyphosate found in the cereal samples using the proposed method. The results were consistent with those obtained by the classical method [34]. To estimate the sensitivity and selectivity for glyphosate detection in cereal samples, we carried out the recovery experiments. The blank cereal samples were spiked with 0.2 and 1.5μgg−1 glyphosate, respectively. The spiked samples were aged for different days (five, fifteen and thirty days) before sample analysis. The glyphosate concentrations in aged samples were detected using standard addition method to avoid any matrix effect. The results are shown in Table 1. The average recoveries of glyphosate in these spiked cereal samples are in the range of89.5–110.0% and the RSD is lower than 3.5%. These results reveal that the proposed method based on IFE is sensitive and reliable for detection of glyphosate in real cereal samples.

In summary, we developed a new strategy for preparing CDs from wool by a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis process. This method provided a simple, facile and green route to fabricate photoluminescent CDs and avoided the complicated post-treatment process. Meanwhile, a novel IFE system between CDs and AgNPs was developed for the determination of glyphosate. Under the optimum conditions, glyphosate could be detected with high sensitivity and low detection limit (12ngmL−1). The established method has been used for glyphosate detection in cereal samples with satisfactory results. Therefore, this simple and reliable assay would possess potential applications in agricultural and environmental fields. However, the method needs further improvement in order to increase the sensitivity and selectivity for glyphosate detection in very low concentrations and the presence of complicated matrixs in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Development Program of the Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (Grant number 20150204070GX).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.07.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
